perturbation.original_text: For a period of 5 years from the date of this Agreement, the receiving party shall not publish, disseminate nor disclose any Proprietary/Confidential Information received from any of the other parties to any third party accept to those of its own employment having valid need to know the information in the course of employment and such disclosure shall be on terms not less restrictive than those herein contained.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party shall not publish, disseminate nor disclose any Proprietary/Confidential Information received from any of the other parties to any third party except to those of its own employment.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 5-year confidentiality period and the requirement for equally restrictive disclosure terms weakens the protection of proprietary information. This could be problematic if trade secrets are involved.
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act protects trade secrets indefinitely as long as they remain secret and provide economic value. By removing the 5-year limitation and restrictive disclosure terms, the modified agreement fails to adequately protect trade secrets, potentially allowing for their misappropriation after the (now non-existent) 5-year period, contradicting the Act.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract includes a 5-year confidentiality period and requires equally restrictive disclosure terms. The changed text removes these protections, weakening the protection of proprietary information. The Law Explanation indicates this potentially contradicts the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which protects trade secrets indefinitely as long as they remain secret and provide economic value. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites California Code, CIV 3426, referencing the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which the change arguably undermines by removing temporal and protective restrictions. This omission creates a legal contradiction since it lessens the protection afforded to trade secrets under California law.

perturbation.original_text: The receiving party shall use the same degree of care to avoid disclosure or use of the Proprietary/Confidential Information as it uses in respect of its own information of like importance but in no case less than a reasonable degree of care.
perturbation.changed_text: The receiving party shall use a minimal degree of care to avoid disclosure or use of the Proprietary/Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'the same degree of care... but in no case less than a reasonable degree of care' with 'a minimal degree of care' dramatically reduces the obligation to protect confidential information. This increases the risk of negligent disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act implicitly requires a reasonable standard of care to protect trade secrets. Reducing the required care to a 'minimal degree' could be interpreted as a failure to take reasonable measures to prevent misappropriation, thus contradicting the Act's intent to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies 'the same degree of care... but in no case less than a reasonable degree of care,' while the changed text mandates only 'a minimal degree of care.' The Law Explanation suggests that the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act implicitly requires a reasonable standard of care. The changed text, by requiring only a 'minimal degree of care,' directly contradicts this implied requirement for reasonable care, potentially failing to prevent misappropriation of trade secrets. The scraped law snippet identifies California Civil Code Title 5 as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Therefore, reducing the standard of care to 'minimal' creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event the disclosing party of such Proprietary/Confidential Information orally discloses the information to the receiving party, the disclosing party agrees to promptly notify the receiving party of the confidentiality of such oral disclosure and reduces to writing such Proprietary/Confidential Information and submit the same to the receiving party within 15 days of such oral disclosure, upon which the receiving party shall not be bound by the confidentiality obligations as herein provided as regards the said Proprietary/Confidential Information disclosed orally.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event the disclosing party orally discloses the information to the receiving party, the receiving party shall not be bound by the confidentiality obligations as herein provided as regards the said Proprietary/Confidential Information disclosed orally.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for the disclosing party to follow up oral disclosures with written confirmation effectively eliminates protection for orally disclosed confidential information. This makes it much easier for the receiving party to claim they were unaware of the confidential nature of the information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act, misappropriation includes the use of trade secrets that one knows or has reason to know are trade secrets. By removing the requirement for written confirmation of oral disclosures, the agreement undermines the ability of the disclosing party to establish that the receiving party knew or had reason to know the information was confidential, thereby weakening trade secret protection and potentially contradicting the Act.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The original text provided a mechanism for the disclosing party to protect orally disclosed confidential information by requiring written confirmation. The changed text removes this requirement, thus weakening the disclosing party's ability to prove the receiving party was aware the information was confidential. The Law Explanation states that this undermines the ability to establish that the receiving party knew or had reason to know the information was confidential, potentially contradicting the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act, because misappropriation includes using trade secrets that one knows or has reason to know are trade secrets. The first snippet identifies California Civil Code section 3426 as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which the edited text now undermines.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that necessary authorizations, permits or licenses including expert licenses as may be required will be obtained prior to the exportation/disclosure of any Proprietary/Confidential Information relating to the technology of the other party. The disclosing party shall notify the receiving party of the need to obtain any required authorizations, permits and licenses and/or the need to comply with any relevant laws or regulations relating to the disclosure. The disclosing party shall obtain the required authorizations, permits and licenses.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that necessary authorizations, permits or licenses as may be required will be obtained prior to the exportation/disclosure of any Proprietary/Confidential Information relating to the technology of the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the explicit requirement for the *disclosing* party to obtain export licenses and notify the *receiving* party shifts the responsibility vaguely. While the receiving party is still nominally responsible, removing the disclosing party's obligations creates ambiguity and could lead to violations of export control laws if neither party takes action.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Export Administration Regulations (EAR) place obligations on *all* parties involved in the export of controlled items, including technology and technical data. Removing the disclosing party's explicit duty to obtain licenses and notify the receiving party could lead to a violation of the EAR if the export occurs without the necessary licenses, as both parties have a responsibility to ensure compliance. Although the general statement remains, the clearer responsibilities have been omitted, increasing the risk of non-compliance.

perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly required the disclosing party to obtain necessary authorizations and notify the receiving party. The changed text removes the disclosing party's responsibilities, potentially leading to non-compliance with export control laws. The Law Explanation states that the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) place obligations on all parties involved in exporting controlled items. Although the general agreement for authorizations remains, the omission of the disclosing party's specific duties increases the risk of non-compliance. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 15 CFR Part 732, addresses steps for using the EAR. This suggests the necessity of following export control regulations, and the omission directly undermines the obligation for *both* parties to take action.
